summary: Shapiro argues that startups should figure out their marketing before building their product. Inspired by Amazon's practice of writing press releases before development, he proposes testing demand through low-cost marketing artifacts like Kickstarter videos, mock app store pages, and blog posts. The marketing that resonates becomes a north star for product development, clarifying which features matter most. He admits he hasn't tried this approach himself yet but plans to.
key_points: If you already have an app and are just starting to think about marketing, you're probably too late — great products are built with marketing in mind from day one
Startups should test marketing before building: create rough Kickstarter videos, mock app store pages, run ads, and write press releases to gauge real interest
The marketing artifact that generates excitement becomes your north star, telling you what's most important about your product
This approach forces feature prioritization: features that excited people get built, those not in the pitch get cut
money_quotes: If you already have the app, and are just now starting to think about marketing, you're probably hosed.
Great apps, services, websites, and the like are built with the marketing in mind from day one.
If you are creating a new startup, you should figure out your marketing first.
Once you find something that resonates, you have a north star.
It's not just that you've figured out how to market your product, it's that you now know what's most important about your product.
Staring at a blank screen it can be hard to know where to start – now you know.
Features that excited people get written. Those that aren't part of your pitch get cut.
key_insight: Marketing isn't just how you sell your product — it's how you discover what your product should be, so do it before you build anything.
